teaching_arc.entry_point: Start from the visible mapping result students want to achieve.
teaching_arc.progression: Explain the SLAM loop, show what inputs matter, then connect that to simulation behavior and checks.
teaching_arc.destination: Students should finish able to narrate what the SLAM system is doing as the map is built.

audience_level: introductory autonomous mobile robotics students learning ROS 2 through lab-first workflows
topic_goal: Help students understand the SLAM loop in simulation well enough to interpret mapping behavior instead of treating it like magic.
topic_takeaways: Students should understand the roles of motion, sensing, and map updates in SLAM.
Students should know what good versus bad mapping behavior looks like in simulation.
Students should be able to explain the conditions that help or hurt SLAM quality.
style_notes: Keep the tone hands-on and confidence-building.
Connect every concept to what students will debug in a terminal, simulation, or robot lab.
Use visualization and mental models before deep command details.
Keep the mapping loop visible in the narration.
Use map and scan visuals to ground the explanation.
transition_style: Move from the mental model to the concrete lab step students will run next, then close each section by explaining what to verify.

slide_ref.source_title: SLAM in Simulation: Cartographer + SLAM Toolbox

slides.narration_seed: Today they must learn both pipelines, not only one. For the assignment, they should create maps with both methods and compare them.

slides.transition_to_next: Carry this idea forward into What you must be able to do after this block so students feel the lesson progressing intentionally toward the topic goal.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.

slides.story_hint: Keep SLAM in Simulation: Cartographer + SLAM Toolbox connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand how SLAM in Simulation: Cartographer + SLAM Toolbox sets up the lesson and why the topic matters.

slide_ref.source_title: Cartographer vs SLAM Toolbox — very basic difference

slides.narration_seed: Compare the alternatives or concepts on Cartographer vs SLAM Toolbox — very basic difference clearly, emphasize the trade-off, and end with the decision rule students should remember.
must_cover: Cartographer = default TurtleBot3 sim path; tuning is mainly in a Lua file
SLAM Toolbox = strong 2D Nav2 workflow; many settings appear as ROS 2 parameters
SLAM Toolbox is easier to inspect with ros2 param ... while running
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Cartographer is powerful but more configuration-heavy to tune deeply
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from What you must be able to do after this block into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.
slides.transition_to_next: Use the next slide to show the consequence of this contrast in a more concrete example or visual.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.
slides.explanation_style: Contrast the alternatives on Cartographer vs SLAM Toolbox — very basic difference explicitly, then end with the simplest decision rule students should keep.
slides.story_hint: Keep Cartographer vs SLAM Toolbox — very basic difference connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should clearly remember the contrast at the center of Cartographer vs SLAM Toolbox — very basic difference and know which side fits which situation.

slide_ref.source_title: Look inside the /map topic

slides.narration_seed: `/map` is not just a picture. It is a message with metadata plus a big row-major data array.
must_cover: Look for info.resolution, info.width, info.height, and info.origin
The long data array stores occupancy values cell-by-cell
-1 usually means unknown
Values near 0 are free; values near 100 are occupied
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Look inside the /map topic to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Look inside the /map topic is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
likely_student_confusion: Students may memorize ROS 2 terms without seeing the actual data-flow relationship between them.
slides.explanation_style: Define Look inside the /map topic in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.

slide_ref.source_title: How /map becomes the image you save later

slides.narration_seed: Build intuition for How /map becomes the image you save later using the visual or mental model first, then connect that picture back to the exact technical idea students need.
must_cover: resolution = meters per grid cell
width, height = number of cells
origin = real pose of cell (0,0)
The saved .pgm is a picture of those grid values

gx, gy = grid cell indices
origin_x, origin_y, resolution come from the map metadata
If you click directly in RViz, RViz already gives you a pose in the map frame

slide_ref.source_title: How to move the robot to get a better looking map

slides.narration_seed: The robot should not race. Smooth motion gives more stable matching.
must_cover: Move slowly and avoid sudden spins
Show the laser clear corners, wall edges, and door frames
Do loop-backs: revisit known areas to help loop closure
Avoid clipping furniture tightly; keep a little clearance

slide_ref.source_title: GIMP cleanup tutorial (Part 1): open and edit safely

slides.narration_seed: Pencil is better than brush because brush makes soft edges and anti-aliasing. We want crisp map cells, not blurred edges.
must_cover: First make a backup: map_toolbox.pgm → map_toolbox_backup.pgm
Open GIMP, then open the .pgm map image file
Zoom in a lot: 400%–1600% so you can edit individual pixels
Use the Pencil Tool, not the Brush tool, for sharp 1-pixel edits
slides.transition_from_previous: Use GIMP cleanup tutorial (Part 1): open and edit safely to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once GIMP cleanup tutorial (Part 1): open and edit safely is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define GIMP cleanup tutorial (Part 1): open and edit safely in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep GIMP cleanup tutorial (Part 1): open and edit safely connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain GIMP cleanup tutorial (Part 1): open and edit safely accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: GIMP cleanup tutorial (Part 2): what to fix, what not to fix

slides.narration_seed: If they change image size or orientation, the YAML metadata may no longer match. This is a last-resort cleanup step, not map redesign.
must_cover: Okay: reconnect a tiny broken wall edge or remove a tiny black/white speck
Not okay: invent new walls, erase real obstacles, or close open doors
Do not crop, rotate, resize, blur, or scale the image
Save the edited .pgm; keep the YAML matched to the same map geometry
slides.transition_from_previous: Use GIMP cleanup tutorial (Part 2): what to fix, what not to fix to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once GIMP cleanup tutorial (Part 2): what to fix, what not to fix is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.

slide_ref.source_title: Important SLAM Toolbox parameters (part 1)

slides.narration_seed: If `resolution` is too coarse, thin features may vanish. If too fine, the map looks detailed but costs more.
must_cover: resolution → smaller cells = finer detail, more memory/compute
min_laser_range → ignore very near noisy returns near the robot body
max_laser_range → ignore very far returns that may be weak or unstable
Tell students exactly what to watch for in the visual or interactive material.
minimum_travel_distance → robot must move this far before updating
slides.transition_from_previous: Shift from explanation into a visual or interactive view of Important SLAM Toolbox parameters (part 1) so students can watch the concept, not just hear it.
slides.transition_to_next: After the visual explanation, step back out and explain the decision rule or takeaway students should keep.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.
slides.explanation_style: Teach through the visual or interactive material on Important SLAM Toolbox parameters (part 1) by narrating what students should notice and why it matters.
slides.story_hint: Keep Important SLAM Toolbox parameters (part 1) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand what to watch for on Important SLAM Toolbox parameters (part 1) and why that visible behavior matters technically.
slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slide_ref.source_title: Important SLAM Toolbox parameters (part 2)

slides.narration_seed: If map updates feel slow in RViz, map update interval may be large. Loop closure is one of the big reasons a map can straighten itself after revisiting an area.
must_cover: map_update_interval → how often the occupancy map is regenerated
do_loop_closing → revisited places can be matched to reduce drift
scan_topic → usually /scan; wrong topic means no mapping data
Tell students exactly what to watch for in the visual or interactive material.
transform_publish_period → how often map -> odom is published
slides.transition_from_previous: Shift from explanation into a visual or interactive view of Important SLAM Toolbox parameters (part 2) so students can watch the concept, not just hear it.
slides.transition_to_next: After the visual explanation, step back out and explain the decision rule or takeaway students should keep.
likely_student_confusion: Students may watch the map update without understanding which signals are driving the update.
slides.explanation_style: Teach through the visual or interactive material on Important SLAM Toolbox parameters (part 2) by narrating what students should notice and why it matters.
slides.story_hint: Keep Important SLAM Toolbox parameters (part 2) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should understand what to watch for on Important SLAM Toolbox parameters (part 2) and why that visible behavior matters technically.
slides.scene_hint: Use a map, scan, and robot pose overlay so students can connect localization or planning state to what they see on screen.

slide_ref.source_title: How to inspect Cartographer tuning files

slides.narration_seed: We are tuning the copy in `~/ros2_ws/src`, not hunting under `/opt/ros`. That makes their own workspace the source of truth for experiments.
must_cover: Go to your workspace source tree:
Then open the file you found:
Typical workflow: edit file → relaunch Cartographer → compare maps

slide_ref.source_title: Important Cartographer parameters (part 1)

slides.narration_seed: `missing_data_ray_length` affects how empty space is represented when the scanner does not get a valid hit. Enabling online correlative scan matching can improve local alignment, but it can also cost more computation.
must_cover: min_range → ignore scans too close to the robot
max_range → ignore scans too far away
missing_data_ray_length → assumed ray length when no obstacle is hit
use_imu_data → use IMU if available and reliable
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Important Cartographer parameters (part 1) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Important Cartographer parameters (part 1) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Important Cartographer parameters (part 1) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Important Cartographer parameters (part 1) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Important Cartographer parameters (part 1) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: Important Cartographer parameters (part 2)

slides.narration_seed: If optimization runs more often, the map may improve sooner but computation rises. If match score thresholds are too low, false matches become more likely; too high and good loop closures may be missed.
must_cover: motion_filter.max_angle_radians → skip tiny-angle scan updates
optimize_every_n_nodes → how often global optimization runs
constraint_builder.min_score → minimum score for accepting a match
global_localization_min_score → trust threshold for global matches
slides.transition_from_previous: Use Important Cartographer parameters (part 2) to introduce the next key term students need before the lesson can move forward.
slides.transition_to_next: Once Important Cartographer parameters (part 2) is clear, use the next slide to apply or test the term instead of leaving it abstract.
slides.explanation_style: Define Important Cartographer parameters (part 2) in plain technical language, then attach one practical robotics consequence or example.
slides.story_hint: Keep Important Cartographer parameters (part 2) connected to the lab workflow students will use later in ROS 2 and mobile robotics.
slides.delivery_goal: Students should be able to explain Important Cartographer parameters (part 2) accurately and use it in the next step of the lesson.

slide_ref.source_title: How to tune Cartographer vs SLAM Toolbox

slides.narration_seed: Compare the alternatives or concepts on How to tune Cartographer vs SLAM Toolbox clearly, emphasize the trade-off, and end with the decision rule students should remember.
must_cover: SLAM Toolbox: inspect with ros2 param list/get/describe, then try live changes
Cartographer: edit the .lua file, relaunch, drive the same path, compare
Change one parameter at a time and keep notes
State the contrast clearly and finish with the practical decision rule.
Save map files with different names so you can compare outputs later
slides.transition_from_previous: Move from Important Cartographer parameters (part 2) into a direct contrast so students can see the trade-off rather than treating the ideas separately.

slide_ref.source_title: Nav2 preview: run navigation with a saved map

slides.narration_seed: Define Nav2 preview: run navigation with a saved map in plain language, then connect the term to one practical robotics consequence so students do not memorize it in isolation.
must_cover: Launch the world first, then run Nav2 with one saved map:
Or use your Cartographer YAML instead
In RViz: set 2D Pose Estimate first, then send a navigation goal
Next session: AMCL, costmaps, planners, controllers, and goal sending
